title: Multihop gateway choice for private deploy: peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN
preview:
From: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.com>
To: Sylvia Park <sylvia@edgecorp.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-04-21T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Multihop gateway choice for private deploy: peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN

Hi Sylvia / Rajiv —

Thanks again for walking through EdgeCorp's on-prem + cloud hybrid plan yesterday. I sketched three practical ingress options we discussed and attached a quick topology diagram and a short matrix. The ask from your side was: which approach minimizes blast radius, meets your compliance needs (no public ingress), and keeps tail-latency under 80ms for your US region app servers.

Quick context: your control plane will run in the customer VPC (us-east-1), and inference pods will be in a dedicated Redwood VPC. We need to choose between:
- VPC Peering (direct routing)
- AWS PrivateLink / GCP/VPC Service Controls (endpoint model)
- Site-to-Site VPN (IPSec) with NAT/multihop gateway

High-level recommendation up-front: PrivateLink if you need strong network isolation (no route table exposure) and per-subnet endpoint control; Peering if you want lowest egress latency and simple pathing and your CIDR overlap can be avoided; S2S VPN only if you require on-prem-to-cloud encrypted tunnels for regional branches or cannot provision cross-account peering due to org policy.

Questions for you before we lock the design:
1) Any hard requirement on source IP preservation (do you need the original client IP inside your network for auth/logging)?
2) Will you provision a unique CIDR for Redwood-dedicated VPC (non-overlapping)?
3) Are there any corporate egress inspection appliances we must route through?

I attached a labeled sketch and a comparison spreadsheet (security, manageability, outage modes, failover playbook).

Next steps: If you confirm (1)-(3), Marcus and Jordan will produce an annotated runbook and a canary plan (we usually do a stage-gated rollout: Privatelink -> small pilot -> promote or fall back to peering if throughput demands it).

Thanks,
Dylan

--
Dylan Brooks
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
[d] +1 415-555-0199
[Attachment: 20270421-edgecorp-topology-sketch.pdf (PDF)]
[Attachment: redwood-privatelink-peering-vpn-comparison.xlsx (XLSX)]

From: Sylvia Park <sylvia@edgecorp.com>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.com>
Cc: Rajiv Menon <rajiv@edgecorp.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-04-21T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Multihop gateway choice for private deploy: peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN

Thanks Dylan — useful framing. A few quick answers:
1) We do need source IP preservation for our rate-limiting proxies and WAF logs.
2) We can provision a non-overlapping /20 for Redwood-dedicated VPC in us-east-1.
3) Yes — all egress to the internet goes through an inspection cluster in our perimeter VPC (we call it egress-gw).

Traffic profile: 95th percentile tail latency target is 80ms; expected concurrent requests to model endpoints ~120 qps in steady state for pilot.

Constraint: security team prefers to avoid expanding route tables across accounts if we can achieve equivalent isolation with endpoints. They also asked if PrivateLink would let them use their egress-gw appliance for outbound calls from the model nodes.

Can you and Marcus confirm the tradeoffs on:
- preserving source IP with PrivateLink (any proxy/nat implications)
- throughput/connection limits for PrivateLink endpoints (we are concerned about TCP connection scale)

Sylvia

--
Sylvia Park
Head of Cloud Platform, EdgeCorp
[s] sylvia@edgecorp.com


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Sylvia Park <sylvia@edgecorp.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-04-22T10:24:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Multihop gateway choice for private deploy: peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN

Sylvia —

Short answers first:
- PrivateLink: by design the endpoint is an ENI in the service consumer account, so source IP that reaches the service (Redwood) will be the endpoint's ENI IP; you will lose original client IP unless you preserve it at the application layer (X-Forwarded-For) or implement proxying that forwards the original IP. For your WAF/rate-limit use-case you could terminate at the consumer side and forward headers over the PrivateLink connection, but we’d need to ensure mutual TLS and header integrity.
- Throughput: AWS PrivateLink scales well for many workloads, but each connection uses ENIs and has soft limits. For 120 qps steady-state with bursts, PrivateLink is feasible — we recommend provisioned endpoints across multiple subnets and a small account quota increase before pilot.

Tradeoff matrix (very condensed):
- Peering: preserves client IP (if routed), lowest network hop count -> best latency. Requires CIDR non-overlap and careful route table hygiene. Exposure: route propagation across accounts must be scoped.
- PrivateLink: strong isolation (no route-table sharing), easier cross-account setup (service consumer creates endpoint), but source-IP visibility lost unless app-layer forwarding is implemented. Good for compliance teams who don't want cross-account routes.
- S2S VPN: good for on-prem branches and encrypting between on-prem and cloud, but higher latency, and more operational overhead; prone to flapping unless redundant tunnels and BGP are configured.

Operational playbook options:
1) If preserving source IP is mandatory and you can allocate non-overlapping CIDRs: Peering as primary, PrivateLink as fallback for restricted subnets (e.g., compliance workloads).
2) If security policy forbids route-table exposure: PrivateLink primary + a lightweight application header-forwarding proxy for IP preservation + aggressive connection pooling to handle scale.
3) If on-prem branches require direct access to the deployed model nodes (for low-latency regional inference), add S2S VPN with dynamic BGP to on-prem and treat the VPN for regional branch egress while keeping peering/privatelink for cloud-to-cloud traffic.

I'll sketch a minimal pilot configuration and a firewall/NAT callout for your egress-gw consideration — will drop a separate runbook by EOD tomorrow.

Marcus

--
Marcus Lin
Principal Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood-privatelink-peering-vpn-comparison.xlsx (updated)]

From: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
To: Sylvia Park <sylvia@edgecorp.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-04-23T15:04:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Multihop gateway choice for private deploy: peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN

Sylvia — quick technical note from an infra perspective:

If you choose PrivateLink and need to preserve source IP for WAF/rate-limiting, a pragmatic pattern we use is a small, consumer-side proxy fleet (two AZs) that terminates TLS, records the original client IPs in X-Forwarded-For, and then connects to Redwood endpoints over the PrivateLink. That proxy can run in the same subnet as your egress-gw so outbound inspection still sees the proxied traffic. Latency added by the proxy is typically <5ms if we colocate it in the same AZ and use ENI-accelerated instances.

On connection scale: configure long-lived HTTP/2 or multiplexed gRPC where possible. For HTTP/1.1 workloads we push pooling at the proxy layer to reduce new TCP handshakes; for high QPS, peering will have the fewest scalability surprises.

I'll also include packet-flow diagrams in the runbook so your network team can eyeball NAT rules. Happy to run a short perf test (1 hour ramp) in our staging if you want measured p99 numbers for each topology.

—Jordan

Jordan Blake
Network Architect, Redwood Inference

Subject: Next steps: integration priorities & MAP sketch

Thanks again for the call earlier — good alignment from our side. Quick recap and asks so we can turn the MAP into an actionable build plan:

- Priority features: 1) SDK embed for our clinician portal (low-latency streaming for live notes), 2) Tool-calling to triage and surface structured outputs (ICD codes + disposition), 3) Embeddings for search ranking later phase.
- Must-haves: EU data residency (eu-west-1), SAML SSO, audit logs retention 1 year.
- Procurement note: our finance/procurement team needs an estimated integration timeline and a list of technical dependencies to start a purchase request.

Can you send a MAP draft with milestone owners + estimated dates so I can escalate internally? Also flag any blockers (e.g., engineering time, security questionnaires). I attached my quick notes from today.

Samir
Director of Platform Integrations
Beacon Health

title: FastCart: field canonicalization plan + CAIQ delta submission
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morris <elena@fastcart.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 16 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: FastCart: field canonicalization plan + CAIQ delta submission

Elena —

Thanks again for the walkthrough this week. We did a quick export of the FastCart portal fields and put together an initial mapping to Redwood's CAIQ/CA documentation. See attached field-map-v1.csv (columns: portal_field, suggested_canonical, example_value, notes).

Two asks from our side to move this along:
- Can you confirm the mapped portal_field names for the items under \"Deployment & Network\" and \"Data Retention\"? Our export flagged several variants (e.g., \"retentionPeriod\" vs \"data_retention_days\").
- FastCart limits portal edits to scheduled maintenance windows — can you share the earliest slot we could push the field-name harmonization? We want to align the portal change with our CAIQ delta so the values land in the same weekly snapshot.

Plan I'm proposing (tentative):
1) We canonicalize the field list and push the small renames in the portal (low risk)
2) Mina prepares a CAIQ delta package reflecting the canonical fields + sample artifacts
3) We submit the CAIQ delta (as a single delta commit) on the next allowed window (you mentioned once per week/quarter constraints)

Attaching: field-map-v1.csv (draft) — I zipped a sample CAIQ export for internal review: CAIQ-delta-commit.zip (will follow once we have alignment).

If this sounds OK I can loop in engineering for a short change window doc and propose Monday/Tuesday windows.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0198

---
Attachment: field-map-v1.csv (CSV, 14 rows)
Attachment: placeholder-CAIQ-delta-commit.zip (stub)

From: Elena Morris <elena@fastcart.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: FastCart Security <security@fastcart.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 16 Apr 2027 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FastCart: field canonicalization plan + CAIQ delta submission

Ben —

Appreciate the clear mapping. Quick notes after a skim of field-map-v1.csv:
- Deployment & Network: our portal exposes two values — \"network_classification\" (internal) and \"exposure_profile\" (customer-visible). For CAIQ purposes, we prefer we map to \"network_classification\" and expose a small computed attribute for downstream queries.
- Data Retention: we currently have both a numeric field (data_retention_days) and a text descriptor (retention_policy_label). The numeric field is canonical internally — the label is derived.

Maintenance windows: we have a short slot on Mon 2027-04-19 01:00–03:00 UTC for low-risk metadata updates (no DB migration). That aligns with your proposed Monday/Tuesday.

One operational constraint: FastCart's security team only accepts CAIQ deltas as a signed bundle via our portal artifact ingest. We can accept a single delta per calendar week; if there's a conflict you may need to queue.

I'll confirm the portal field IDs and attach the raw export by EOD.

Thanks —
Elena Morris
Security Program Manager, FastCart
elena@fastcart.com

Attachment: fastcart-portal-export-20270416.json (stub)

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 19 Apr 2027 08:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FastCart: field canonicalization plan + CAIQ delta submission

Folks —

Thanks Ben/Elena for the context. Internal ops notes and proposed split of responsibilities:

Summary of constraints:
- FastCart accepts one signed CAIQ delta bundle per week and requires the portal canonical names to be present in the artifact.
- Their maintenance window on Mon 2027-04-19 01:00–03:00 UTC is suitable for metadata-only edits (no downtime expected).

Action plan (recommended):
1) Jordan: coordinate with FastCart to reserve the 01:00–03:00 UTC slot and confirm the portal-field IDs Elena will provide. Please capture their artifact ingest URL and signing requirements. (Owner: Jordan)
2) Mina: take the attached field-map-v1.csv, normalize the header names to CAIQ canonical names, and generate the CAIQ delta bundle (JSON + signature). Target draft by EOD Sun 2027-04-18 so we can review Monday morning. (Owner: Mina)
3) Ben: confirm customer commit and any commercial notes (we may want to note the window in the mutual action plan).

Risk & rollback: because these are metadata updates, rollback is trivial, but we should keep a hashed snapshot and include the pre-change export in the delta bundle.

Also: Mina, please include a short explanatory README inside CAIQ-delta-commit.zip describing field derivations (e.g., why we map retention_policy_label -> data_retention_days).

If we hit the weekly delta limit, plan B is to stage changes in the portal and deliver the signed delta on the next available slot; that's not ideal for the compliance snapshot but workable.

Kimberly Park
Security Intake Lead | Redwood Inference
kimberly_park@redwood.com

title: Demo time confusion around NZ/US DST change — please confirm
preview:
From: Sophie Lloyd <sophie.lloyd@fintechx.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Nguyen <tom@fintechx.com>
Date: Sun, 14 Mar 2027 21:12:00 +13:00
Subject: Re: Demo availability — proposed Mar 21 10:00 NZDT

Hi Chloe / Marissa,

Thanks again — we would love to lock in the demo. We were talking internally and the team leaned toward the slot you suggested: Mar 21 at 10:00 NZDT. However, I'm worried there might be a mismatch because of the NZ DST (UTC+13) and the US daylight savings changes earlier this month — I want to make sure our attendees in APAC and the US see the same wall-clock time.

I've attached an .ics with the original slot and a quick CSV I exported from our calendar showing attendee timezones (attached). Can you confirm this maps to the right US times for your presenters? If there's any ambiguity we can pick an alternate window.

Attachments:
- proposed_slots.ics (calendar invite stub)
- slot-conversions.csv (spreadsheet stub)

Thanks,
Sophie
Sophie Lloyd
Head of Integrations, FintechX
Sophie.lloyd@fintechx.com

On Sun, Mar 14, 2027 at 08:15 +13:00 Tom Nguyen <tom@fintechx.com> wrote:
> Sophie: 10am NZDT is fine for our folks but double-check with Redwood — I think the US side might be getting tripped by the recent clock change.
>
> -- Tom


From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
To: Sophie Lloyd <sophie.lloyd@fintechx.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 14 Mar 2027 09:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Demo availability — proposed Mar 21 10:00 NZDT

Hi Sophie — thanks for flagging this. Short answer: we can run the demo at a time that works for both APAC and the US, but there are two different DST transitions in play and they do create ambiguity in some calendar tools.

Quick notes / options:
- NZDT on Mar 21 is UTC+13.
- The US Pacific side has already moved to DST this month (UTC-7). Many calendar clients will display both correctly, but when meeting creators are in different zones some clients (and calendar exports) sometimes show the wrong offset.

To avoid confusion, I recommend either:
1) Confirm the meeting in NZ local time (we host at 09:00 or 10:00 NZDT) and we include explicit UTC times in the invite and the meeting title (e.g., "09:00 NZDT / 18:00 UTC / 11:00 PT").
2) Pick an APAC-first window earlier in the day for NZ and ask US attendees to join late afternoon UTC.

Concrete proposals (all Mar 21):
- Option A (APAC-friendly): 09:00 NZDT / 20:00 AEST / 18:00 UTC / 11:00 PT
- Option B (Middle-ground): 07:00 NZDT / 18:00 AEST / 16:00 UTC / 09:00 PT

I've attached the slot-conversions.csv with the conversions I ran locally — can you confirm which you'd prefer? If you want, I'll create a single calendar invite that includes the UTC string in the title so there's no ambiguity.

Jordan (cc'd) — once Sophie confirms which option works, can you send the calendar invite and include our Zoom link?

Chloe Martin
Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference
chloe.martin@redwood.ai
Attachment: slot-conversions.csv (conversion table, stub)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Sophie Lloyd <sophie.lloyd@fintechx.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Mar 2027 11:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Demo availability — proposed Mar 21 10:00 NZDT

Thanks Chloe — quick confirm from infra/ops side. I ran the conversions locally and here's a clean table you can paste into the invite: 

- 09:00 NZDT (UTC+13) = 20:00 AEST (UTC+10) = 18:00 UTC = 11:00 PT (UTC-7)
- 10:00 NZDT (UTC+13) = 21:00 AEST = 19:00 UTC = 12:00 PT
- 07:00 NZDT = 18:00 AEST = 16:00 UTC = 09:00 PT

Recommendation: pick 09:00 NZDT (first row) as it keeps APAC in morning and US attendees in mid/late morning PT — less late-night for APAC and not too early for the US west coast. We'll also record the session as a backup for any EU team members who can't join.

If you'd like me to create the invite instead of Jordan I can. Also note: include the literal UTC string in the event title (e.g., "Mar 21 — 09:00 NZDT / 18:00 UTC") to avoid client UI mismatch.

—Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Director, Product Partnerships
Redwood Inference
Attachment: calendar_screenshot.png (screenshot stub showing conversions in my calendar)


From: Sophie Lloyd <sophie.lloyd@fintechx.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Nguyen <tom@fintechx.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 14 Mar 2027 16:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Demo availability — confirmed 09:00 NZDT

Perfect — 09:00 NZDT on Mar 21 works for our core group.

Please send the calendar invite with the UTC string in the title and include a Zoom link; we'll accept it from there. If anything changes we'll reply quickly but otherwise see you then.

Thanks and appreciate the quick coordination,
Sophie

preview:
From: Sally Pearson <sally.pearson@acme-corp.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
Cc: audits@acme-corp.com
Date: Mon, 1 Mar 2027 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: isolation topology and boundary narrative for SOC 2 / SIG

Hi Chloe,

Thanks for taking our SOC 2 / SIG request. As part of our infrastructure assessment for the upcoming audit, we need a short packet describing how Redwood handles network and tenancy isolation for Dedicated and Private deployments. Specifically, could you provide: 

- A high-level network diagram showing control plane vs data plane separation for Dedicated and Private modes (annotated with zones/subnets/VLANs).
- A one-page boundary narrative describing tenant isolation guarantees and egress restrictions.
- A segregation matrix mapping controls to SIG/CAIQ items we flagged (we have a shortlist attached to the HS ticket).
- Any attestations or signed statements you can share about air-gapped/on-prem options and KMS/HSM boundaries.

Our timeline is tight: draft packet by EOD Wed (2027-03-03) would be ideal. Please let me know if that is feasible and who on your side we should expect artifacts from.

Thanks,
Sally
Audit Program Manager, Acme Corp

[Attachment: acme_audit_flags.csv]

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Sally Pearson <sally.pearson@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 1 Mar 2027 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: isolation topology and boundary narrative for SOC 2 / SIG

Hi Sally — thanks for the request and timeline. We can deliver a draft packet by EOD Wed. I am looping in Ben and Kim for the topology + segregation matrix; Jordan will coordinate the packaging and signatures.

Quick notes so we align on scope: 
- Ben will produce an annotated PDF diagram (control vs data plane, egress choke points, edge demarcation).
- Kim will compile the mapping to SIG/CAIQ items and produce the segregation_matrix_acme.xlsx.
- Jordan will add the one-page boundary narrative and package any attestations (we can provide a standard attestation letter for Private deployments; for fully air-gapped on-prem customers we provide a tailored statement).

Ben / Kim / Jordan — can you confirm ETA and whether you need anything else from Acme (e.g., list of services they plan to run on Dedicated vs Private)?

Chloe
Principal Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Office: +1 415 555 0123



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 2 Mar 2027 10:34:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: isolation topology and boundary narrative for SOC 2 / SIG

Chloe — confirming I have the request. Deliverables attached: redwood_enclave_topology_v1.pdf (annotated) and a starter segregation matrix (segregation_matrix_acme.xlsx). Summary below; see attached PDF for visuals.

Summary (technical):
- Deployment modes: Hosted API (shared multi-tenant), Dedicated (reserved GPU pools in customer-designated cloud region), Private (VPC or on-prem with customer-managed networking). This packet focuses on Dedicated and Private.
- Control plane vs Data plane: Control plane (management/orchestration) lives in a hardened control VPC with strict IAM and mTLS between Redwood services; data plane nodes (inference runtime) are provisioned in per-customer subnets for Dedicated and private tenant projects for Private deployments. The PDF annotates the flow.
- Tenant isolation: For Dedicated, we use per-customer VLANs and instance-level tenant tagging; requests are routed through policy gateways that enforce per-tenant prefix/KV cache separation. For Private (VPC), tenant resources are deployed into customer-managed networks with private peering; we do not share runtime hosts across unrelated customers unless explicitly contracted.
- Egress & firewalling: Default deny egress with explicit egress proxies for telemetry and model downloads. Egress to customer endpoints is controlled via customer-managed NAT or dedicated egress nodes for Dedicated.
- KMS/HSM boundary: Customer-managed KMS is supported for Private; Redwood-managed KMS backed by HSM is used for hosted/dedicated when customers opt in. Key usage is logged and mapped in the segregation matrix.
- Attestations: We can provide a standard attestation letter confirming the above controls; Jordan will coordinate signatures.

Attachments included (stubs):
- redwood_enclave_topology_v1.pdf (diagram: control/data plane, subnets, egress, peering)
- segregation_matrix_acme.xlsx (mapping: SIG domains -> Redwood controls; columns: control_id, description, evidence_link)

Drive sources: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1a2b3cEXAMPLE (diagrams + editable source files)

Ben

title: Loss avoidance: price mapping, technical carveouts and migration credits
preview:
From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Lang <avery.lang@weatherlyhealth.com>, Sam Patel <sam.patel@weatherlyhealth.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Intro + quick ask on economics mapping after demo

Hey Avery / Sam —

Thanks for the time today. Appreciate the candid run through of the platform and the migration concerns you flagged (especially the egress window and model parity during pilot). As discussed, I wanted to follow up with a concise price-mapping exercise so your procurement team can see an apples-to-apples comparison to your incumbent.

Attached: redwood_competitive_match_3up.xlsx — three quick packaging ideas (compact, standard, fleet) with our suggested match anchors and assumptions.

Two asks to keep us synced:
- Can you share the incumbent line-items you need matched (runtime minutes vs. seat vs. committed throughput)? A spreadsheet export is ideal.
- Are there any technical carveouts (e.g., private VPC egress exceptions, custom PKI) that will keep us from matching on day one?

If you'd like, Jordan (cc) and I can draft a narrow, conditional match that includes a migration credit tied to pilot velocity — keeps things safe for Weatherly and keeps Redwood from open-ended credits. We're aiming for a minimal path to procurement sign-off, not a long negotiation.

Best,
Connor
Connor O'Brien
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com | +1 415-555-0192

Attachment: redwood_competitive_match_3up.xlsx (spreadsheet, 3 sheets)

From: Avery Lang <avery.lang@weatherlyhealth.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sam Patel <sam.patel@weatherlyhealth.com>, Procurement Team <procurement@weatherlyhealth.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2027 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Intro + quick ask on economics mapping after demo

Connor —

Thanks for the recap and the attached workbook. Quick answers:
- Procurement wants runtime minutes and committed token throughput (monthly) broken out. They will also want an egress estimate line so we can project network charges.
- Technical carveouts: we have two items that may require additional controls before you can match fully: 1) FIPS-compliant KMS wrapping for keys in our PHI workflows; 2) an exception window for patient-data retention (we have a 90-day archival requirement).

On the commercial side, the team will consider a time-limited migration credit if Redwood can guarantee that the pilot meets three rollout KPIs (latency < 200ms p95 for our 3k token flows, model parity on our top-10 prompts, and rollout within 60 days of contract signature). If you can give a simple conditional term sheet, we can expedite to our legal and procurement.

We can provide the incumbent export tomorrow.

Thanks,
Avery
Avery Lang
Head of ML Product, Weatherly Health
avery.lang@weatherlyhealth.com | +1 617-555-3810

Attachment: (will send incumbent_export.csv separately)

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2027 08:24:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Intro + quick ask on economics mapping after demo (internal)

Connor / Marissa —

Saw Avery's note. Quick internal take: we should propose two narrow offers rather than a single be-all match:

1) Compact Match (low-risk): match incumbent unit price for committed runtime minutes for 6 months, no SLA extension, include a one-time pilot migration credit equal to 25% of estimated first-month spend (capped). No changes to KMS; operate under our standard managed keys but offer a documented integration plan for FIPS wrap (deliverable within 30 days of SOW).

2) Aggressive Match (conditional): full parity on line items including egress estimate + 60-day pilot credit that steps down by 50% post-pilot if KPIs are met. Requires carveout: we accept FIPS wrap but need a 30-day integration milestone to avoid open-ended commitments. Also add a rollback clause if model parity fails in the pilot.

I've sketched numbers in the attached workbook (sheet "Condensed Offers"). I'd recommend we lead with Compact Match to avoid procurement pushback.

-Jordan
Jordan Blake
Director, Strategic Accounts
jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com

Attachment: redwood_competitive_match_3up.xlsx (refer to "Condensed Offers")

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Lang <avery.lang@weatherlyhealth.com>, Sam Patel <sam.patel@weatherlyhealth.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Feb 2027 15:40:00 -0800
Subject: Commercial term sheet: compact bundle + conditional migration credit

Avery / Sam —

Following the team's internal review, please find a compact, legal-friendly term sheet attached that we think maps cleanly to the incumbent and isolates the two technical carveouts you mentioned. Highlights below:

- Compact Bundle: 12-month commitment, matched unit pricing for committed runtime minutes (line-item parity), monthly egress estimate included as a pass-through.
- Migration Credit: One-time credit equal to 25% of the first billed month (capped at $50k). Credit triggers if the three pilot KPIs are met within the 60-day pilot window.
- Technical carveouts: We commit to provide a FIPS-wrapped KMS integration plan and a documented data-retention mode that meets your 90-day archival requirement. Both are contractual milestones with acceptance criteria and 30/30/30 delivery windows.
- SLA / Escrow: We can provide an elevated SLA for latency (p95 target) but want an integration milestone before extending financial penalties.

Attachment: weatherly-compact-bundle-term-sheet.pdf (2 pages). Also re-attached the pricing workbook for transparency.

title: Grafana/Datadog connector - proxy & auth handshake
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sara Milton <sara.milton@grafana.com>, partner-ops@datadog.com
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 9 Feb 2027 10:12:00 -0800
Subject: Grafana/Datadog connector - proxy & auth handshake

Hi Sara / Datadog Partner Ops,

Quick intro: Redwood is building a managed connector that allows Grafana Agent -> Redwood runtime -> Datadog exporter in customer VPCs (common ask for regulated customers). A few customers hit a blocker around corporate proxy deployments and mutual auth between the agent and the exporter.

We'd like to align on supported auth modes and recommended proxy patterns before we finalize the marketplace package. Specific asks:
- Which proxy auth flows do you recommend for Grafana Agent when the outbound is to an internal Datadog collector (mTLS, token passthrough, or OAuth delegation)?
- Are there Datadog-supported intermediary proxy configurations that preserve trace/span integrity and headers?
- Any known gotchas for air-gapped or egress-restricted environments (CA bundles, hostname verification)?

I've attached a scribble of the proposed flow (auth_flow_diagram.png) and a draft mutual action plan (mutual-action-plan.xlsx). Can we get a short technical sync (30m) this week with the infra owners so we can lock the connector auth matrix?

Thanks — cc'ing Laura (platform integrator) and Neha (security/compliance).

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Partnerships | Redwood Inference
+1 415-555-0132

Attachments: auth_flow_diagram.png (image), mutual-action-plan.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Sara Milton <sara.milton@grafana.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Tim Huang <tim.huang@datadoghq.com>, partner-ops@datadog.com
Date: Tue, 9 Feb 2027 13:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Grafana/Datadog connector - proxy & auth handshake

Thanks Ben — good context.

Short answers up front:
- Datadog export can be configured for token-based auth or mTLS. mTLS is preferred for zero-trust setups; token passthrough works for customers that centralize creds in the agent.
- For proxies we generally recommend a transparent TLS-forwarding proxy that preserves SNI and client certs. If the proxy terminates TLS, you must re-establish mTLS on the proxy->collector leg — that is a common source of header loss.
- Air-gapped customers: ensure CA bundles include Datadog collector CAs and validate hostname overrides if using internal DNS. We often see failures when proxies rewrite Host headers.

Two asks from our side:
1) Can you share a minimal config snippet for Grafana Agent in the proposed flow? That will help our engineering team reproduce the path.
2) If you have a list of customers that need the air-gap pattern, mark them in the M-A-P so we can prioritize packaging.

I can do a 30m sync Thurs 11am PT. Looping in Tim from Datadog to confirm collector behavior in proxied setups.

—Sara
Sara Milton
Partner Solutions Engineer, Observability Integrations
Grafana Labs

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Feb 2027 09:02:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Grafana/Datadog connector - proxy & auth handshake

Forwarding Sara's reply and Tim's inclusion. Laura — can you pick this up as the technical owner? Key items to drive in the sync:
- Minimal Grafana Agent config snippets for mTLS and token modes (Sara asked for this).
- Validation tests we can run in our staging: header passthrough, CA bundle injection, hostname override.
- If we choose mTLS, outline automated cert rotation approach and key rollout path for marketplace artifacts.

Neha — flagging potential compliance questions for air-gapped customers (key escrow, CA pinning). Jordan — please scope the change to the connector packaging (config templates + install script).

Suggested sync: Thu 11am PT with Sara + Tim + us. I'll send calendar invite once you confirm.

Thanks,
Ben

title: QBR compressed flightcheck: readiness, launch windows, and top risks
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Priya Rao <priya@synapselabs.ai>
Date: 2027-02-08T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: QBR compressed flightcheck: readiness, launch windows, and top risks

Team / Priya,

As promised, sending a short, focused snapshot ahead of Friday's QBR prep call. Goal: align product/eng status to the customer-facing launch timeline and highlight the three highest-impact delivery risks we need to close this week. Attachments: QBR_readiness_sheet_v2.xlsx (single-sheet status), launch-risk-heatmap.png.

High level:

title: Fintrax discovery — model fit recap, regression Qs, and pin options
preview:
From: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.ai>
To: Claire Sun <claire@fintrax.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 18 Jan 2027 09:02:00 -0800
Subject: Fintrax discovery — model fit recap, benchmark links, next steps

Claire — thanks again for taking the time this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we don’t lose momentum:

- What we heard: Fintrax needs a productionized summarization + entity-extraction pipeline for transaction narratives (high precision on AML-risk flags, low hallucination tolerance). Priority: accuracy at scale, deterministic behavior under updates.

- Recommended models to evaluate (based on today's prompts & latency targets):
  1) redwood-open-xxl-q4 (8-bit quantized) — best baseline for cost/throughput.
  2) redwood-hrl-1.3 (higher-recall, slightly higher cost) — for edge cases in AML patterns.
  3) redwood-td-small (fast, for low-latency inline checks) — to combine with cascade logic.

- Deliverables I’ll share today: evaluation prompt set (attached), benchmark summary for the test corpus we discussed, and an eval diff we ran comparing redwood-open-xxl-q3 -> q4 showing small shifts on a subset of entity labels.

Next steps proposed:
1) Your team to confirm a prioritized prompt subset (we attached our initial selection).
2) Redwood to run a short regression analysis against your sample data and flag any quality regressions + suggested pin/rollback options.
3) Schedule a 30-min triage with engineering if regressions look material.

I attached the prompt set and the benchmark PDF. I also pasted a short snippet of the q3->q4 diff in the evaluation pack where we saw differences in low-frequency merchant codes.

If it helps, we can pin Fintrax's production route to redwood-open-xxl-q3 while we run the tests — that avoids customer-facing drift. Let me know if procurement wants an internal note on what pinning means operationally.

Best,
Diego Fernandez
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
---
Attachments: fintrax_prompt_set_v1.json (prompt set, 24 prompts) ; benchmark_summary_202701.pdf (6 pages)


From: Claire Sun <claire@fintrax.ai>
To: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 18 Jan 2027 12:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Fintrax discovery — model fit recap, benchmark links, next steps

Thanks Diego — this is helpful. A couple quick clarifying questions from our side: 

1) The diff you mentioned (q3->q4) — how large was the sample and can you surface concrete examples of the entity-label regressions? Our compliance team will need those examples to evaluate risk.

2) If we pin to q3, what is the operational impact for updates and security patches? We can’t be on an unsupported runtime for long.

3) Timeline: Procurement wants to understand how long the eval/regression phase will take before we sign a POC commitment.

Could you loop in engineering so we can get a short SLA and a concrete rollback/pin playbook?

Thanks,
Claire
Head of Data Products — Fintrax
Fintrax Solutions


From: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Cc: Claire Sun <claire@fintrax.ai>
Date: Mon, 18 Jan 2027 13:15:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Fintrax discovery — model fit recap, benchmark links, next steps

Team — forwarding Claire's follow-ups. Action items: 

- Ben: can you produce a short excerpted diff (3–5 concrete examples) that shows the q3->q4 label changes Claire asked for and a short note on whether this signals a distribution shift vs nondeterministic token differences? Attach a snippet we can share with compliance.
- Jordan (CS): please draft a one-page pin/rollback playbook for Fintrax explaining operational implications (security/patching, how long pinning is supported, how we do emergency rollbacks). Procurement needs this for their POC approval.
- Marissa: help keep timeline tight — Claire asked about eval duration. Recommend making the short regression run (their sample) 3 business days; flag if you think we need more.

I’ve attached the prompt set and the benchmark summary we already sent. Ben — please attach the diff and any suggested mitigations when you reply.

Diego
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

---
Original message quoted for context.


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Cc: Claire Sun <claire@fintrax.ai>
Date: Tue, 19 Jan 2027 07:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Fwd: Fintrax discovery — model fit recap, benchmark links, next steps

Diego / team / Claire — attaching the requested artifacts and a short technical note. Summary up front:

- Sample size & methodology: diff was run on 2,500 transaction narratives (the same seed set we discussed). The q3->q4 comparison looked for label changes on named-entity extraction (merchant, merchant_category_code, country) and a small set of AML-risk flags.

- Concrete examples (excerpted):
  a) Input: "Payment to ACME TECH.SVC - code 5932"
     q3 -> merchant_category_code: 5932 -> 5734 (incorrect)
     q4 -> merchant_category_code: 5932 -> 5932 (correct)
     (In some low-frequency tokens q3 had a false positive that q4 corrected; in other cases the inverse happened.)

  b) Input: "TRANSFER TO LUNA INTL - beneficiary country: Unknown"
     q3 -> country: Unknown (no extraction)
     q4 -> country: "United States" (spurious fill)
     (This is a rare span-fill behavior triggered by certain prompt templates.)

  c) Input: "REFUND FROM MTR LOGISTICS 14-07"
     q3 -> merchant: "MTR LOGISTICS"
     q4 -> merchant: "MTR" (truncated entity — impacts reconciliations)

- Interpretation: these are low-frequency, prompt-dependent regressions — not wholesale model collapse. Risk to Fintrax is real for those edge cases you flagged.

- Options to recommend:
  1) Pin production to redwood-open-xxl-q3 while we run a full regression suite on your full sample (operational: pin preserves runtime patching; we still apply critical security patches to q3 branch for up to 90 days).
  2) If you need updates, run a canary set on 10% traffic routed to q4 with strict monitoring and automatic fallback on entity-drift thresholds.
  3) Short-term mitigation: apply prompt normalization rules in the pipeline (we included a sample preprocessor in the eval pack).

Attachments included:
- eval_diff_patch.txt (excerpted diffs and redacted examples)
- fintrax_prompt_set_v1.json (prompt set we will use for the regression run)
- benchmark_summary_202701.pdf (high-level numbers)

Ben Carter
Senior ML Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

---
Forwarded for Claire's compliance review; happy to join a triage call if useful.

  1) Pin production to redwood-open-xxl-q3 while we run a full regression suite on your full sample (operational: pin preserves runtime patching; we still apply critical security patches to q3 branch for up to 90 days).
  2) If you need updates, run a canary set on 10% traffic routed to q4 with strict monitoring and automatic fallback on entity-drift thresholds.
  3) Short-term mitigation: apply prompt normalization rules in the pipeline (we included a sample preprocessor in the eval pack).

Attachments included:
- eval_diff_patch.txt (excerpted diffs and redacted examples)
- fintrax_prompt_set_v1.json (prompt set we will use for the regression run)
- benchmark_summary_202701.pdf (high-level numbers)

Ben Carter
Senior ML Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

---

title: Private Offer: prepaid voucher + reseller margin for StellarBio
preview:
From: Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Structuring private offer — prepaid voucher + channel routing

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week. Procurement here at StellarBio is evaluating a private offer on AWS and we wanted to explore a hybrid structure: a prepaid voucher (commit) that Stellar purchases up-front for a 12-month term, with the ability to route fulfillment and invoicing through our reseller, CloudReseller.

Key asks from procurement:
- Can Redwood support an upfront voucher that behaves like a committed credit (redeemable against usage)?
- How would reseller margin be handled (pass-through vs. list-price holdback)?
- Any constraints for AWS private offers around invoice routing and tax handling?

Tom Wei from procurement is looped for finance details. If possible, could you send an initial sketch of mechanics and a rough impact to list pricing + reseller margin?

Appreciate it — Lucia
Purchasing Manager, StellarBio

--
On Thu, Dec 10, 2026 at 08:45 AM PST Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote: (previous thread snippet)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>, Tom Wei <t.wei@stellarbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Dec 2026 11:04:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Structuring private offer — prepaid voucher + channel routing

Hi Lucia / Tom —

Thanks for the clear list. Short answers up-front: yes, we can support a prepaid voucher-style commitment, and we can route fulfillment via a reseller, but the routing and margin mechanics depend on the marketplace (AWS vs GCP vs Azure) and invoice requirements.

Attached is a short draft that sketches two options:
1) Voucher as prepaid committed credit (Stellar buys credit; credits applied to usage against the private-offer SKU). Reseller receives a pass-through fee on redemption (recommended for tax simplicity).
2) Voucher + list-price holdback (Redwood invoices Stellar at a discounted list, reseller is paid from a separate settlement flow). This is possible but adds reconciliation overhead and longer settlement windows.

I've included a quick margin calc (voucher_margin_breakdown.xlsx) to show net cash to reseller vs Redwood ARR recognition impact. High-level notes:
- AWS private offers: we can be the seller of record or route via CloudReseller as the seller depending on the PO setup. Seller-of-record simplifies invoicing but changes reseller margin flow.
- For a prepaid voucher, we recommend a 12-month expiry and monthly burn reporting (dashboard).

Jordan — can you confirm ops feasibility for seller-of-record vs partner-seller flows and expected lead time?
Karthik — can you flag recommended margin guidance (cap or % band) for Stellar-sized deals?

Next step: I'll pull legal to draft a short SOW once you confirm which option you prefer.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
attachments: Stellar_PrivateOffer_Draft.pdf, voucher_margin_breakdown.xlsx


From: Tom Wei <t.wei@stellarbio.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>
Cc: CloudReseller Ops <partnerops@cloudreseller.com>
Date: Fri, 11 Dec 2026 08:20:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Structuring private offer — prepaid voucher + channel routing

Ben — thanks for the options. Procurement preference is Option 1 (voucher as prepaid committed credit) because it keeps Stellar's spend predictable. A few clarifying questions from finance:
- For invoicing, Stellar needs a single invoice that includes the prepaid voucher charge. Is Redwood able to issue that invoice with CloudReseller noted as the fulfillment partner?
- CloudReseller needs a clear monthly settlement file showing redemptions and their margin percentage for accounting. Can you produce a CSV export from the console?
- Any concerns about expiry / unused credit treatment from a revenue recognition perspective?

CloudReseller Ops — please confirm whether a 6% reseller fee is acceptable to you and if you need gross-vs-net handling.

Tom Wei
Senior Procurement Analyst, StellarBio


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Tom Wei <t.wei@stellarbio.com>, Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Dec 2026 09:33:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Structuring private offer — prepaid voucher + channel routing

Tom / Lucia — looping in ops and legal. Quick answers from ops:
- Invoicing: We can issue a single Redwood invoice for the prepaid voucher. AWS private offer metadata can include the reseller assignment, but for seller-of-record scenarios Redwood will invoice Stellar directly and concurrently push a settlement file to CloudReseller.
- Settlement files: We will generate a monthly CSV with: date, redemption_amount_usd, sku, invoice_id, reseller_fee_pct, reseller_payment_due_date. We can include a transaction ID to match AWS billing if required.
- Unused credit: Standard practice is to include a 12-month expiry with a 90-day pro-rata refund window for bona fide business reasons; otherwise unused credit is recognized as breakage per ASC 606 — we'll share suggested language for the order form.

Karthik — pricing: for a $250k prepaid voucher we recommend a 6% reseller fee in a pass-through flow. If Stellar needs a larger margin, suggest moving to a list-price holdback but note that adds ~30–45d reconciliation and potential tax nuances. Can you confirm internal approval for 6% for deals sized 200–500k?

Irene — can you provide the short clause for the order form re: expiry + breakage treatment?

Action items:
- Ben: finalize order form option selection with Stellar.
- Jordan: send a sample settlement CSV template and ops SLA.
- Irene: provide 2–3 sentence legal language for expiry/breakage.

Jordan Blake
Partner Ops | Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lucia Ramos <lucia@stellarbio.com>, Tom Wei <t.wei@stellarbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>, CloudReseller Ops <partnerops@cloudreseller.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Dec 2026 16:48:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private Offer: prepaid voucher + reseller margin for StellarBio

Thanks everyone — appreciate the quick turnaround. Lucia / Tom, based on your preference for predictability, recommended next step is:
1) Confirm Option 1 (voucher prepaid credit) and accept the 12-month expiry with a 90-day pro-rata refund exception.
2) Confirm reseller fee at 6% (pass-through) so CloudReseller can receive monthly settlements without changing seller-of-record.
3) I'll create an order form + SOW draft with the legal clause from Irene and include Jordan's settlement CSV template.

If you confirm in the next 48 hours, we can have a draft order form for review by Thursday and aim for a Dec 31 invoice date to hit Q4 recognition for Stellar.

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Phone: (415) 555-0193
ben.carter@redwood.com
attachments: voucher_margin_breakdown.xlsx

> On Mon, Dec 13, 2026 at 09:33 AM Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com> wrote:
> Jordan's ops notes attached above.

If you can provide a redacted roster and a roadmap for full evidence delivery that would be helpful. We have a deadline for committee review on Dec 20 — can you confirm whether you can deliver by then and what the handoff mechanism will be (secure upload, SFTP, or shareable Drive link)?

title: Clarify: private-offer margin contingency and escrow routing for ClearWave
preview:
From: Sarah Li <sarah@clearwave.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Tue, 8 Dec 2026 16:12:00 -0800
Subject: Private offer — margin contingency + escrow routing questions

Ben —

Thanks again for the demo last week. Finance ran the numbers on the private offer and we have a couple of operating questions before we finalize the PO (attached). High level:

- We expect a short period of over-commitment in Q1 (usage could be ~15% above the committed SKU). We want to confirm how Redwood will handle oversubscription for channel payouts — is there a margin contingency that reduces partner pass-through when usage exceeds the commit?
- On the settlement side, our procurement/legal team requires an escrow for a portion of the credit while the usage true-up happens. Can you point me to Redwood’s preferred escrow flow (vendor holding vs. marketplace escrow) and whether that affects list price / channel margin?

Attached is the draft PO (ClearWave_PO_v2.pdf) and our suggested repayment timing. We’re trying to avoid a manual month-by-month workback with your partner — any standard playbook you have would be great.

Would you be able to loop in the partner/channel ops and legal folks who handle private-offer payment routing? Ideally we’d get an answer this week so procurement can sign.

Thanks,
Sarah Li
Director, Finance — ClearWave

Attachment: ClearWave_PO_v2.pdf


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>
To: Sarah Li <sarah@clearwave.com>
Cc: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.inference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Dec 2026 08:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private offer — margin contingency + escrow routing questions

Sarah —

Appreciate the clarity and the PO. Looping in Irene (legal & marketplace contracts) and Jordan (channel ops) so we can give you definitive routing guidance. Quick answers up front:

1) Oversubscription and partner payout: Redwood’s standard private-offer playbook is to credit the customer for overage at the agreed overage rate and to compute partner pass-through on net revenue post-crediting. We don’t automatically reduce the partner share for short-term overages, but we do have a margin contingency clause for sustained overages beyond a defined threshold (usually >20% for two consecutive billing periods). If you expect ~15% temporary oversubscription, that sits below our contingency trigger.

2) Escrow and settlement routing: We prefer marketplace escrow when the customer is purchasing via a cloud marketplace (AWS/GCP/Azure) because it preserves the standard billing and settlement cadence. If you need vendor-side escrow (ClearWave wants funds held with a third party until true-up) we can support it but it requires a short addendum — that addendum typically clarifies who bears any settlement fees and whether the list price is considered ‘gross’ for channel pass-through calculations. Vendor-side escrow can shift timing of partner payouts but does not change the contracted list price.

Irene / Jordan — can you confirm the standard wording we’d use for a vendor-side escrow addendum and whether a temporary 15% overage should be treated as normal overage (no partner reduction) under CW-2026-PO-4821?

Next steps: If you’re okay with marketplace escrow, we can proceed without addendum; if not, we’ll send a short escrow addendum and a sample payout waterfall for partner accounting.

— Ben
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference


From: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.inference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>, Sarah Li <sarah@clearwave.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private offer — margin contingency + escrow routing questions

Ben / Sarah —

Adding legal perspective. Short version: vendor-side escrow is contractually feasible but we need to record the escrow in an addendum that references the private-offer ID and the payout waterfall. Key points we include in the addendum:

- Definition of escrowed amount and trigger for release (e.g., net true-up completed within 60 days post-billing).
- Which party pays escrow agent fees (typically customer by default; negotiable).
- Confirmation that channel margin is calculated on net realized revenue after credits/true-ups unless parties explicitly agree otherwise.
- Contingency clause: temporary oversubscription (<20% for up to two billing cycles) will not reduce partner pass-through. Sustained oversubscription may prompt a renegotiation of pass-through percentage, not an automatic haircut.

Sarah — I’ve attached Redwood’s standard escrow addendum template and a short partner payout waterfall we use in marketplace private offers. If you want vendor-side escrow we’ll prepare the addendum and route to your procurement for signature; it’s usually a 24–48 hour turnaround internally once we have confirmation on who bears escrow fees.

Attachment: redwood-escrow-template.docx

Best,
Irene Choi
Head of Legal, Marketplace & Compliance
Redwood Inference


From: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.inference.com>
To: Sarah Li <sarah@clearwave.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>, Irene Choi <irene@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Dec 2026 11:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private offer — margin contingency + escrow routing questions

Sarah —

Quick operational note from channel ops. If you opt for marketplace escrow (recommended for speed):

- Settlement timeline: vendor payouts follow the marketplace schedule (typically net-45 from invoice date).
- Partner margin posting: we post the partner share to the partner ledger after marketplace settlement; any credits from usage true-ups are reflected on the next settlement cycle.
- Example waterfall (for CW-2026-PO-4821): Gross list price -> marketplace fees -> customer credits/true-ups -> net realized revenue -> partner pass-through (agreed %).

If you go vendor-side escrow, expect a one additional settlement cycle delay while we reconcile with your escrow agent. Also, let us know any specific reporting fields your AP team needs; we can add partner-specific line items to the invoice metadata to simplify reconciliation.

If that works, Ben and I will prepare the short escrow addendum and a sample payout schedule and drop into your HubSpot deal and DocuSign link for signature.

Thanks — Jordan
Channel Operations Manager
Redwood Inference

We don’t automatically reduce the partner share for short-term overages, but we do have a margin contingency clause for sustained overages beyond a defined threshold (usually >20% for two consecutive billing periods). If you expect ~15% temporary oversubscription, that sits below our contingency trigger.

2) Escrow and settlement routing: We prefer marketplace escrow when the customer is purchasing via a cloud marketplace (AWS/GCP/Azure) because it preserves the standard billing and settlement cadence. If you need vendor-side escrow (ClearWave wants funds held with a third party until true-up) we can support it but it requires a short addendum — that addendum typically clarifies who bears any settlement fees and whether the list price is considered ‘gross’ for channel pass-through calculations. Vendor-side escrow can shift timing of partner payouts but does not change the contracted list price.

Irene / Jordan — can you confirm the standard wording we’d use for a vendor-side escrow addendum and whether a temporary 15% overage should be treated as normal overage (no partner reduction) under CW-2026-PO-4821?

Next steps: If you’re okay with marketplace escrow, we can proceed without addendum; if not, we’ll send a short escrow addendum and a sample payout waterfall for partner accounting.

— Ben
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference


From: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.inference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>, Sarah Li <sarah@clearwave.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private offer — margin contingency + escrow routing questions

Ben / Sarah —

Adding legal perspective. Short version: vendor-side escrow is contractually feasible but we need to record the escrow in an addendum that references the private-offer ID and the payout waterfall. Key points we include in the addendum:

- Definition of escrowed amount and trigger for release (e.g., net true-up completed within 60 days post-billing).
- Which party pays escrow agent fees (typically customer by default; negotiable).
- Confirmation that channel margin is calculated on net realized revenue after credits/true-ups unless parties explicitly agree otherwise.
- Contingency clause: temporary oversubscription (<20% for up to two billing cycles) will not reduce partner pass-through. Sustained oversubscription may prompt a renegotiation of pass-through percentage, not an automatic haircut.

Sarah — I’ve attached Redwood’s standard escrow addendum template and a short partner payout waterfall we use in marketplace private offers. If you want vendor-side escrow we’ll prepare the addendum and route to your procurement for signature; it’s usually a 24–48 hour turnaround internally once we have confirmation on who bears escrow fees.

Attachment: redwood-escrow-template.docx

Best,
Irene Choi
Head of Legal, Marketplace & Compliance
Redwood Inference

Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>, Irene Choi <irene@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Dec 2026 11:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private offer — margin contingency + escrow routing questions

Sarah —

Quick operational note from channel ops. If you opt for marketplace escrow (recommended for speed):

- Settlement timeline: vendor payouts follow the marketplace schedule (typically net-45 from invoice date).
- Partner margin posting: we post the partner share to the partner ledger after marketplace settlement; any credits from usage true-ups are reflected on the next settlement cycle.
- Example waterfall (for CW-2026-PO-4821): Gross list price -> marketplace fees -> customer credits/true-ups -> net realized revenue -> partner pass-through (agreed %).

If you go vendor-side escrow, expect a one additional settlement cycle delay while we reconcile with your escrow agent. Also, let us know any specific reporting fields your AP team needs; we can add partner-specific line items to the invoice metadata to simplify reconciliation.

If that works, Ben and I will prepare the short escrow addendum and a sample payout schedule and drop into your HubSpot deal and DocuSign link for signature.

Thanks — Jordan

title: Contract-edit orchestra: roles, sprint dates, and redline queue
Admin activity log extraction, sampling & redaction plan
preview:
From: Rachel Dunn <rachel.dunn@medicosys.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
Cc: BrightShield Audit <soc2@brightshield.com>, Legal Team <legal@medicosys.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Hi Camila,

Thanks for taking this on. As part of our SOC 2 audit (scope: user and admin activity) BrightShield has asked for a timebound extract of admin/API/operator activity for the period 2026-06-01 to 2026-11-30. Topline asks:

- Full change events (who/what/when) for role changes, API key creates/revokes, model deployments, permission grants
- Operator session start/stop, console sign-ins, and impersonation events
- Timestamps in UTC, event IDs, and request IDs for correlation
- Preferred delivery: compressed Parquet OR newline-delimited JSON (JSONL) with a manifest and checksums

BrightShield also wants to understand our sampling approach if we provide a rolling sample instead of full export, and confirmation of PII redaction applied to free-text fields before delivery. Can you confirm what you can provide, expected delivery cadence, and what attachments (schema/manifest) you will include?

Thanks,
Rachel
Rachel Dunn
Security Compliance Lead, MedicoSys

[Attachment: request_summary.pdf]


From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Rachel Dunn <rachel.dunn@medicosys.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-02T10:04:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Hi Rachel — appreciate the clear ask. Summary of what Redwood can deliver and proposed hygiene: 

- What’s logged (exportable fields): event_ts_utc, event_id, actor_id, actor_email, actor_role, action_type, resource_type, resource_id, request_id, ip_address (hashed on request), user_agent, kv_changes (structured, limited to metadata keys), kv_cache_hit (bool), model_version, deployment_id, trace_id. 
- Retention & scope: we retain admin/operator activity for 18 months. The requested window (2026-06-01 to 2026-11-30) is fully available.
- Export formats supported: Parquet (preferred for size), JSONL, and CSV. We will supply a manifest (snapshot_manifest_20261205.jsonl) + SHA256 checksums (runbook_checksums.txt).
- Redaction options: we can apply token-level PII redaction to free-text fields or redact entire free-text columns. We will not alter event IDs/request IDs or timestamps.
- Sampling: default is full export; if you elect sampling, we support deterministic stratified sampling by actor_role + day to preserve event distribution. Recommend sampling only for dev/test evidence, not for primary SOC2 runs.

I’ve attached the admin_activity_schema_v1.csv and a short export_options_matrix.xlsx that maps size/transfer methods (S3 presigned, SFTP, encrypted transfer appliance).

Proposed next steps:
1) Confirm delivery format (Parquet vs JSONL) and PII redaction level by EOD 2026-12-03.
2) We will prepare a dry-run manifest and checksum for your review.
3) Final export scheduled for 2026-12-05 with transfer details to follow.

Edge notes: if Legal needs an attestation letter regarding redaction steps, loop us in and we’ll produce a short statement.

Thanks — Camila
Camila Reyes
Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference

[Attachments: admin_activity_schema_v1.csv, export_options_matrix.xlsx]

From: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Rachel Dunn <rachel.dunn@medicosys.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-03T08:19:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Adding ops detail from the serving/runtime side: 

- Snapshotting: we will create a point-in-time snapshot for the requested window sampled by event_ts_utc. For large exports we stream into compressed Parquet partitions (by day) and produce a top-level manifest.
- Integrity & custody: manifest will contain per-file SHA256; we can provide PGP-signed manifest on request. For delivery we support:
  * s3 presigned (preferred for size),
  * SFTP push to your firewalled endpoint (we will follow sftp_handshake_instructions.pdf), or
  * managed transfer to an approved cloud storage bucket (cross-account).
- Redaction implementation: redaction runs in a dedicated scrub job that writes both a redacted and a provider-only unredacted version to an airgapped bucket; unredacted copy is retained ONLY for 90 days under custody controls and is deleted after the attestation window unless Legal requests otherwise.

Marcus — please confirm throughput estimate and expected job runtime so we can set a delivery ETA.

— Jordan
Senior Platform Engineer, Redwood Inference

[Attachment: sftp_handshake_instructions.pdf]

From: Rachel Dunn <rachel.dunn@medicosys.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, BrightShield Audit <soc2@brightshield.com>, Legal Team <legal@medicosys.com>
Date: 2026-12-04T11:07:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Thanks Camila and Jordan — this is very helpful. A couple of clarifications before we finalize: 

1) PII redaction: we want deterministic redaction so auditors can validate that the same event redacts consistently across exports. Token-level hashing with a salted (per-export) salt is acceptable if you include the hashing algorithm and salt rotation policy in the manifest.
2) Custody: we need an attestation that unredacted copies are not shared outside Redwood and the 90-day holding/custody process is auditable. Can Legal provide that?
3) Delivery: MedicoSys prefers S3 presigned links (they ingest directly). Please provide a presigned bucket path and an estimated TTL.
4) Manifest: please include row counts per partition and a sample of 10 event_ids (non-PII) for early validation.

If you can confirm the above we will route this into BrightShield's evidence intake queue and schedule the remote validation window.

Regards,
Rachel

On a related note — BrightShield asked whether Redwood logs the KV cache hit/miss flag for generation requests. If you can include that field (kv_cache_hit) that would help a separate traceability check.


From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Rachel Dunn <rachel.dunn@medicosys.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Legal Team <legal@medicosys.com>
Date: 2026-12-05T15:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Rachel — confirmed and scheduling below. Summary (final plan):

- Format & transfer: Parquet, partitioned by day, compressed. Delivery via S3 presigned PUT links. Each file will have SHA256 in the top-level manifest. Example S3 path: s3://redwood-evidence/medicosys/2026-12-05/export-20261205.parquet
- Redaction & determinism: deterministic salted hashing will be applied to actor_email and ip_address fields (salt recorded in manifest, salt rotation policy attached). Free-text columns will be redacted entirely in the delivered export.
- Custody & attestation: Legal will generate an attestation that unredacted copies are retained in a restricted bucket for 90 days and are only accessible to the evidence custodian team. Legal action: @Legal Team please prepare a one-paragraph attestation we can include with the manifest by 2026-12-05 18:00 PST.
- Manifest: will include partition-level row counts, a 10-event sample (event_id + event_ts_utc), SHA256 checksums, and the hashing algorithm details. We will also include kv_cache_hit as requested.
- Delivery ETA: dry-run manifest by 2026-12-04 20:00 PST (uploaded to shared drive), final export and presigned links by 2026-12-05 20:00 PST.
- Follow-ups: we will log the handoff to HubSpot ticket HS-483920 and attach the manifest there.

Action items:
- Camila: prepare manifest + checksums (in progress)
- Jordan/Marcus: run snapshot job and produce Parquet partitions (in progress)
- Legal: attestation paragraph (requested)

If everything looks good I will push final presigned links and the runbook_checksums.txt to the Drive folder and mark HS-483920 as evidence-ready.

Thanks everyone — Camila
Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference

[Attachments uploaded: snapshot_manifest_20261205.jsonl, runbook_checksums.txt]

From: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwoodinference.com>

Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: CAIQ: residency attestation and short runtime-sample for marketplace listing

Sanjay —

Thanks again for the quick sync this morning. As discussed, Neptune's marketplace listing process requires a filled CAIQ plus two supporting artifacts: a short runtime log sample showing token residency (date-limited) and a brief attestation that logging/retention practises meet the marketplace residency requirements.

High-level ask: please confirm whether a 7‑day runtime snapshot (UTC 2026‑11‑08 to 2026‑11‑15) and a signed attestation will satisfy your intake checklist. If the team needs a different window or additional metadata fields (tenant id obfuscation, sequence ID, request path), let me know.

Proposed timeline:
- We produce the runtime sample and attestation draft by EOD 2026‑12‑02
- Neptune does a short technical review and confirms acceptance by 2026‑12‑05

I’m looping in Jordan (marketplace lead) and Marissa (CS) to track the intake and handoff to compliance.

Thanks —
Aditya
Head of Sales Ops
Redwood Inference

Attachments: (none in this message)

From: Sanjay Patel <sanjay@neptunehealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T11:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: CAIQ: residency attestation and short runtime-sample for marketplace listing

Aditya —

Thanks for the clarity. The intake team is okay with a 7‑day window; two quick clarifications before we proceed:

1) Redaction: we require tenant identifiers to be pseudonymized (sha256-token or similar) in the sample.
2) Scope: include only request/response latency headers and residency tag (region code). No payloads.

If you can provide the snapshot in a zipped CSV with the columns: pseudonymized_tenant_id, timestamp_utc, region_code, request_path_stub, token_count, latency_ms that should be perfect.

Also please include a one‑paragraph attestation signed by Redwood legal or CISO that logs were captured and stored in the APAC region for the date range provided. A short DocuSign link is fine for signature.

If you can hit the 2026‑12‑02 timeline, I’ll escalate to the marketplace intake for faster review.

Regards,
Sanjay
Neptune Health — Marketplace Intake

Aditya

title: Evidence mailbag: Bluemint onboarding telemetry and SLA checklist
preview:
From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@bluemint.io>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Evidence mailbag: Bluemint onboarding telemetry and SLA checklist

Hi Jordan / Monica,

Thanks again for the walkthrough earlier this week. Our infra lead (cc'd) asked I share the initial small-run telemetry we captured when we exercised the Redwood endpoint from our staging region yesterday — attaching the CSV and a JSON snapshot.

Quick context: the run was ~2k tokens per request, steady load ~60 qps for 15 minutes. We saw a P95 that drifted to ~420ms and a few tail spikes around 720ms; not sure if that's noisy from our side or indicative of settings we should change on the Redwood side.

Request: can you confirm what a minimal evidence package for onboarding signoff looks like from your side (latency/throughput targets, required telemetry, accepted file formats)? If you need a fuller run we can schedule one next week — let us know preferred window.

Attachments:
- bluemint_load_run_20261120.csv (text/csv)
- telemetry_snapshot_20261120.json (application/json)

Thanks,
Elena Ruiz
Senior SRE, Bluemint Labs
elena.ruiz@bluemint.io



From: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@bluemint.io>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Nov 2026 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Evidence mailbag: Bluemint onboarding telemetry and SLA checklist

Elena — thanks for the artifacts, very helpful. Quick read: the CSV shows steady concurrency but a few >P95 spikes that line up with cold KV cache misses in the snapshot. That can happen on short runs.

Proposed minimal evidence package for Bluemint signoff (what we'll deliver):
- 1x confirmed throughput run (30m) at your target QPS with per-request latencies (CSV)
- Telemetry snapshot including KV cache hit rate, GPU utilization, token distribution (JSON)
- Annotated tail-log (plain text) highlighting any observed anomalies
- SLA checklist doc (spreadsheet) showing measured P50/P95/P99 vs target and gating flags

We can produce those artifacts and drop them into the shared Drive folder. Suggested next steps:
- You confirm target throughput and latency gates (example below)
- We schedule a 30-minute controlled run on Tue/Wed next week (we can run in your staging region)
- We run canary, capture artifacts, and we (Redwood) assemble the acceptance packet for signoff

Suggested example gates (open to adjustments):
- Throughput: sustained 120 qps for 30 minutes
- Latency: P95 <= 300ms, P99 <= 650ms
- Error rate: <0.1% request errors

I attached a working SLA checklist (v0.2) and a short runbook for the canary steps to this thread. If those targets are too aggressive we can iterate. Monica / Karthik — can you confirm who will own the run and telemetry capture on Redwood's side?

Thanks — Jordan
Jordan Blake
Technical Account Manager, Redwood Inference
jordan_blake@redwood.com



From: Carlos Mendes <c.mendes@bluemint.io>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@bluemint.io>
Date: Mon, 23 Nov 2026 08:20:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Evidence mailbag: Bluemint onboarding telemetry and SLA checklist

Jordan / Elena — quick add from our engineering lead: Bluemint's primary goal is to validate production-like traffic from our east-us region. Our target (for M0) is 120 qps sustained with P95 under 300ms — if the P95 needs to be 250ms we'd escalate, but 300ms is acceptable.

Also: we prefer runs in the 03:00–07:00 UTC window to match low-traffic windows on our side. We'll co-ordinate SSH access for the synthetic runner if Redwood needs to fetch client-side traces.

Carlos
Carlos Mendes
Platform Manager, Bluemint Labs
c.mendes@bluemint.io



From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Nov 2026 11:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Evidence mailbag: Bluemint onboarding telemetry and SLA checklist

Jordan / Monica — looping in Neha for telemetry intake and to confirm RX/TX of artifacts. Proposed internal run plan (Redwood-owned):

1) Prep (Day -1): provision a dedicated canary slot in us-east, pre-warm KV cache with a 15m ramp to minimize cold-miss impact.
2) Canary run (Day 0): 30m sustained at 120 qps, run three identical seeds, collect per-request timing, GPU utilization, and kernel selection logs.
3) Post-run (Day 0 + 1): assemble acceptance packet (CSV + JSON + annotated logs + SLA checklist), upload to shared Drive, and circulate to Bluemint for review.

I attached a short runbook (runbook-canary-steps.pdf) and the SLA checklist draft. I'll own the run execution; Neha will own telemetry collection and artifact assembly. Monica — please own the customer-facing signoff and tracking in HS.

On schedule: Tue 2026-11-30 04:00–05:00 UTC (that maps to early US evening for us); Jordan, Monica — is that workable?

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Senior SRE / Onboarding Eng, Redwood Inference
karthik_iyer@redwood.com



From: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@bluemint.io>, Carlos Mendes <c.mendes@bluemint.io>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Nov 2026 15:48:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Evidence mailbag: Bluemint onboarding telemetry and SLA checklist

Hi Elena / Carlos — thanks, all set from our side. Confirming the plan and what you'll receive in the acceptance packet:

What Redwood will deliver (post-canary):
- bluemint_load_run_YYYYMMDD.csv (per-request timing + status)
- telemetry_snapshot_YYYYMMDD.json (KV cache, GPU util, kernel map)
- annotated-tail-run-logs.txt (where we call out anomalies)
- sla-checklist-v0.2.xlsx (measured vs gates, pass/fail flags)

Schedule: Tue 2026-11-30 04:00–04:40 UTC (30m run + 10m buffer). Redwood will pre-warm at 03:45 UTC. We'll upload artifacts to the Drive folder and drop a HubSpot note (HS-234512) once upload completes.

Links / Next actions:
- Shared artifacts folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aB_example_shared_run_artifacts
- HubSpot record: HS-234512 (I'll log artifacts and status)
- Draft SLA amendment (if you want a formal change): https://www.docusign.com/embeddedSigning/example-sla-amendment

Please reply with a single-line ACK to confirm the window and whether Redwood should pull client-side traces (SSH) during the run. If we don't hear back by Mon 2026-11-29 23:59 UTC we'll assume ACK and proceed.

Thanks again — looking forward to getting Bluemint across the finish line.

Jordan Blake
Technical Account Manager, Redwood Inference
jordan_blake@redwood.com
